growth_records.cite: Jacobson et al. In Vivo Thermodynamic Analysis of Glycolysis in Clostridium thermocellum and Thermoanaerobacterium saccharolyticum Using 13C and 2H Tracers. mSystems (2020). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00736-19. [PMC7380578]
growth_records.snippet: Fermentations and growth were carried out in MTC (...5 g/liter either glucose or cellobiose; pH 7 for C. thermocellum...). We used a previously published biomass equation and ethanol excretion rate, together with our measured uptake and exc
growth_records.notes: C. thermocellum grown on cellobiose (5 g/L) in MTC medium, pH 7, 55°C, strictly anaerobic, batch, harvested mid-exponential (OD600 0.40-0.5). Full MTC recipe transcribed verbatim from Methods. Metabolic fluxes derived by COMPLETE-MFA from five parallel 13C/2H tracer experiments and are reported as a PROPORTION of glucose uptake normalized to 100 (dimensionless); absolute specific growth rate and absolute uptake/excretion fluxes (mmol/gDW/h) are in supplementary Table S2, which is not present in the fulltext XML, so no numeric mu or absolute qS/qP could be extracted. Secreted products explicitly listed: lactate, ethanol, acetate, formate. No arithmetic possible; confidence low due to absent numeric rates.

up.method: computed qS = mu/Yxs; = 0.385/0.290 = 1.325 g/gDW/h; /342.3 g/mol x1000 = 3.87 mmol/gDW/h

sec.method: HPLC, slope of product vs cellobiose consumed over exponential phase (OD600 0.4-2.2)
HPLC, slope of product vs cellobiose consumed over exponential phase

growth_records.cite: Kuil et al. Functional Analysis of H+-Pumping Membrane-Bound Pyrophosphatase, ADP-Glucose Synthase, and Pyruvate Phosphate Dikinase as Pyrophosphate Sources in Clostridium thermocellum. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.1128/aem.01857-21. [PMC8863071]
growth_records.snippet: the ethanol yield only slightly increased from 0.81 to 0.90 mol mol-1 and the acetate yield decreased from 1.10 to 0.96 mol mol-1 relative to LL1004; quadruple deletion strain AVM061 showed a 22% and 38% decreased growth rate and biomass yi
growth_records.notes: Wild-type LL1004 grown at 55C, anaerobic, LC medium + 5 g/L cellobiose (Fig 6/Table S10). WT growth rate and biomass yield are NOT printed directly in-text (Table S10 is supplemental); they are DERIVED from stated percentage reductions in the mutant: quad KO AVM061 is stated as 22% lower growth (0.30 h-1) and 38% lower biomass yield (0.18 g/g) than LL1004. mu_WT = 0.30/(1-0.22) = 0.385 h-1; Yxs_WT = 0.18/(1-0.38) = 0.290 g biomass/g cellobiose. Doubling time = ln2/0.385 = 1.80 h. qS(cellobiose) computed = mu/Yxs = 0.385/0.290 = 1.325 g/gDW/h = 3.87 mmol/gDW/h (cellobiose MW 342.3). Ethanol (0.81) and acetate (1.10) yields in mol/mol cellobiose are stated explicitly for LL1004. product_yields ethanol listed as 0.81 in text for WT (0.96 typo in yields field is AVM061's acetate; WT acetate=1.10). Fermentation also measured formate, lactate, pyruvate, malate by HPLC but only ethanol/acetate yields reported in text. Not a 13C-MFA study.

growth_records.strain: AVM061 (delta-ppase delta-Pags1,2-ags1 ags2 delta-ppdk delta-clo1313_1686; quadruple knockout of LL1004)

up.method: computed qS = mu/Yxs = 0.30/0.18 = 1.667 g/gDW/h; /342.3 g/mol x1000 = 4.87 mmol/gDW/h

sec.method: HPLC, slope of product vs cellobiose consumed over exponential phase
HPLC, slope of product vs cellobiose consumed over exponential phase

growth_records.cite: Kuil et al. Functional Analysis of H+-Pumping Membrane-Bound Pyrophosphatase, ADP-Glucose Synthase, and Pyruvate Phosphate Dikinase as Pyrophosphate Sources in Clostridium thermocellum. Applied and Environmental Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.1128/aem.01857-21. [PMC8863071]
growth_records.snippet: Although combinatorial deletion of all four putative PPi sources reduced the growth rate by 22% (0.30 +/- 0.01 h-1) and the biomass yield by 38% (0.18 +/- 0.00 g biomass g substrate-1)
growth_records.notes: Engineered quadruple-knockout strain, same batch condition as LL1004 (55C, anaerobic, LC + 5 g/L cellobiose). mu = 0.30 +/- 0.01 h-1 and Yxs = 0.18 +/- 0.00 g/g are stated directly (abstract + Results). Doubling time = ln2/0.30 = 2.31 h. qS(cellobiose) computed = mu/Yxs = 0.30/0.18 = 1.667 g/gDW/h = 4.87 mmol/gDW/h. Ethanol yield 0.90 and acetate yield 0.96 mol/mol cellobiose stated explicitly. Paper also estimates an internal PPi flux of ~9.7 mmol PPi gx-1 h-1 for this strain (assuming PPi-Pfk is only PPi consumer) - this is an intracellular flux estimate, not an exchange rate, so not listed as uptake/secretion. Not a 13C-MFA study.

growth_records.cite: Roberts SB et al. Genome-scale metabolic analysis of Clostridium thermocellum for bioethanol production. BMC Systems Biology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-4-31. [PMC2852388]
growth_records.snippet: Fermentation experiments in which C. thermocellum grew in continuous culture and consumed cellobiose at 2.2472 mmol gDW-1 hr-1 ... the experimentally measured growth rate was 0.08 hr-1
growth_records.notes: Chemostat at steady state so mu=D=0.08/h. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.08 = 8.66 h (calculated). Cellobiose uptake 2.2472 mmol/gDW/h and mu=0.08/h are EXPERIMENTAL values cited from Hogsett DAL, 'Cellulose Hydrolysis and Fermentation by Clostridium thermocellum for the Production of Ethanol', PhD thesis, Dartmouth University 1995 (ref 32). Acetate, formate, ethanol secretion measured and used as FBA constraints but numeric values appear only in Fig 2A (not in machine-readable text). In silico minimal medium components listed: water, ammonia, sulfate, phosphate, calcium, ferrous iron, hydrogen sulfide, potassium, magnesium, pantothenate, nicotinate D-ribonucleotide.

growth_records.medium: MJ minimal medium (fructose as carbon source)

up.method: measured fructose uptake flux used as FBA constraint; numeric value only in Fig 2B, not in text

growth_records.cite: Roberts SB et al. Genome-scale metabolic analysis of Clostridium thermocellum for bioethanol production. BMC Systems Biology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-4-31. [PMC2852388]
growth_records.snippet: In the case of continuous growth on fructose, the experimentally measured growth rate was 0.125 hr-1 ... Applying a single constraint for fructose uptake flux resulted in a predicted growth rate of 0.123 hr-1
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state so mu=D=0.125/h. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.125 = 5.55 h (calculated). mu=0.125/h is EXPERIMENTAL, cited from Hogsett 1995 PhD thesis (ref 32). Fructose uptake flux was measured and applied as FBA constraint but no numeric value in text (Fig 2B only). Acetate, lactate, ethanol, formate secretion measured and used as constraints; numeric values only in Fig 2B.

growth_records.medium: minimal medium (cellobiose as carbon source), batch

up.method: batch-culture fermentation (measured; cited from Rydzak et al. 2009)

growth_records.cite: Roberts SB et al. Genome-scale metabolic analysis of Clostridium thermocellum for bioethanol production. BMC Systems Biology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-4-31. [PMC2852388]
growth_records.snippet: Fermentation experiments in which C. thermocellum grew in batch culture and consumed cellobiose at 12.8 mmol gDW-1 hr-1 [33] were the basis for testing model predictions of batch growth on cellobiose.
growth_records.notes: Batch cellobiose uptake 12.8 mmol/gDW/h is EXPERIMENTAL, cited from Rydzak T, Levin DB, Cicek N, Sparling R, 'Growth phase-dependant enzyme profile of pyruvate catabolism and end-product formation in Clostridium thermocellum ATCC 27405', J Biotechnol 2009;140:169-175 (ref 33). No experimental growth rate reported for the batch condition in text (model predicts max 0.518/h unconstrained, 0.387/h fully constrained - these are predictions, not recorded here). Fermentation products acetate, formate, ethanol, H2, CO2, lactate, succinate measured and used as FBA constraints; numeric values only in Fig 2C, not in machine-readable text.
